content.text: Webhooks are HTTP callbacks: when an event occurs, the producer sends an HTTP POST (or occasionally PUT) to a URL you register. Agent platforms use webhooks to notify your service when a run completes, a human approves a step, or an external SaaS tool finishes work.
every few seconds, the provider pushes a signed JSON payload to your endpoint. Your handler validates, acknowledges quickly, and processes asynchronously.

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
import hmac
import hashlib

app = FastAPI()
WEBHOOK_SECRET = "whsec_..."

def verify_signature(body: bytes, signature: str | None) -> None:
    if not signature:
        raise HTTPException(401, "missing signature")
    expected = hmac.new(
        WEBHOOK_SECRET.encode(),
        body,
        hashlib.sha256,
    ).hexdigest()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(f"sha256={expected}", signature):
        raise HTTPException(401, "invalid signature")

@app.post("/webhooks/agent-complete")
async def agent_complete(request: Request):
    body = await request.body()
    verify_signature(body, request.headers.get("X-Signature-256"))
    event = await request.json()
    await enqueue_agent_result(event)  # fast ack, slow work in queue
    return {"received": True}

Long agent runs (minutes) do not belong on open HTTP requests. Webhooks decouple execution from notification: the agent runtime finishes, then POSTs results to your integration URL. The same pattern connects Stripe payments, GitHub pushes, and Slack events to automation.
— react when state changes, not on a timer
— must verify signatures and reject replayed payloads
— events may arrive out of order under retries
— harder than synchronous API calls; need delivery logs
You configure a callback URL in the agent platform or SaaS admin UI. Some systems require a challenge handshake (echo a secret) to prove you control the endpoint.

async def handle_webhook(event: dict) -> None:
    event_id = event["id"]
    if await store.seen(event_id):
        return  # duplicate delivery
    await store.mark_seen(event_id)
    await process_agent_result(event["data"])
Providers retry on non-2xx responses with exponential backoff. Return 2xx only after the payload is safely persisted or enqueued — not after full downstream processing if that can fail.
for async agent completion, billing events, CI notifications
when you cannot expose a public URL (local dev) or need strict pull control
for live UI updates to a connected browser, not server-to-server notify

A nightly batch agent processes 10k documents on a vendor cloud. Each job POSTs to https://api.example.com/hooks/agent with run_id, status, and output URL. Your service enqueues indexing work and returns 200 within 200ms. Failed deliveries retry for 72 hours; idempotency keys prevent double indexing.
— timeouts cause retries and duplicate processing
— use transactional outbox or idempotent steps
Verify HMAC or provider-specific signatures on raw body bytes
Respond quickly; process in a background worker or queue
Store event IDs for idempotency with TTL matching retry window
Expose a manual replay tool using provider delivery logs
Use HTTPS only; rotate webhook secrets periodically
Reject timestamps outside a skew window to limit replay attacks
Rate-limit webhook routes separately from public API
Log payload hashes, not full secrets or PII
Test failure paths: return 503 and confirm provider retries
— webhooks push; polling pulls on an interval
— webhooks are HTTP-facing; queues are internal
— webhooks are discrete events, not continuous token flow
Webhooks are doorbell notifications: the agent rings when done; you answer quickly and handle the package inside — you do not stand at the door conducting the entire unpacking.
Webhooks = HTTP POST callbacks for async events
Verify signatures; respond fast; process idempotently
Expect at-least-once delivery and out-of-order events
Ideal for agent run completion and third-party integrations
Not a substitute for live streaming to connected clients
